sections.text: At night, the legendary king Rugomora Mahe lies tossing and turning and worrying about the spiritual state of his kingdom (lines 1-3.3).
At dawn, citizens appear and report wondrously strange events (lines 4-23); they are the result, perhaps, of some disruptive spiritual force.

A snake behaves like a calf (lines 6.2-7), while lizards speak, asking about where to find beer and taunting warriors (lines 9-12). The king wonders and is instructed about the subject of serpents (lines 13-22.1).
The king sends the men to Mafumbo to sacrifice to the spirit connected to the snakes that dwell there (lines 23-28.2)

They arrive at dusk and make their sacrifices (lines 29-30). At dawn they discover a giant tree, and they decide to report it to the king. They envision and enumerate the uses it can serve (lines 30.1-44)
The men return to the king and report, and the king summons artisans and sends them off to cut down the tree (lines 45-60).
[The men travel and arrive at dusk.] They make their sacrifice (lines 61-1.1). At dawn they find the sacrifices gone \'97 taken by the spirit (lines 61.2-63).
The men try to cut the tree, but it refuses to be cut and speaks to them in a blaming and taunting way (lines 64-86).
When the men report to the king, he orders divination to find a way to cut down the tree; he orders gifts for Mugasha to secure his help (lines 88 \'97134.2).
The men paddle on the lake and make sacrifices to spirits (lines 136-149.4).
The king's emissaries go on foot to the home of Thunderstorm, where they receive directions to Mugasha (lines 150-60).
They journey on the lake, find the fisher-god at the lakeshore, and present their gifts; he is much amused (lines 161-179).
Mugasha's wife Nyakalembe appears and Mugasha explains to her the identity of the men and their mission (lines 180-84). Mugasha and Nyakalembe feed the men (lines 185-91).
Mugasha summons his nature-spirit minions (lines 192-208.2).
One by one, the lake-related spirits attend and are praised (lines 209-230.1).
Then one by one they vow great achievement in the battle to come (lines 231-51).
Mugasha provisions them and sets them on their journey (lines 251.1-259). They travel to the tree (lines 260-71).
Each of the nature spirits attacks the tree in its characteristic way, but the tree seems impervious (lines 272-86).
Finally, Thunder Storm attacks with rain and lightning bolts (lines 287-92).
The tree loses a single branch and calls out in pain and anger to the nature spirit. It sends a defiant message to Mugasha (lines 293-300.2).
Frustrated to tears, Thunder Storm brings the report to Mugasha (lines 301-14).
Mugasha makes preparations. He orders his drum to be sounded (lines 315-21.1).
Now other nature spirits arrive to aid him; each is duly praised and then transformed into a part of Mugasha's battle equipment (lines 322-48.2).
Rain attends borne by three different winds and vows to accompany the fisher god (lines 349-355.1).
Mugasha asks his wife Nyakalembe to provision the trip and she does so (lines 356-68).
Nyakalembe decides she will also go to war and chants her praise as a warrior, which turns out to be women's wedding songs (lines 370-89).
The party sets out and journey's across the lake (lines 390-5).

Mugasha pauses on his journey to mediate a dispute between two villages and another one between two mountains by physically shoving them apart, changing the archaic landscape to its present form (lines 396-411).
Then he creates five named places for people to draw water, the penultimate one also being associated with his wife (lines 413-18.1).
He stops to mediate the dispute of another pair of mountains (lines 419-27).
He happily continues on his journey, cutting passages between mountains and one across a river as he goes (lines 428-37).
He rests, asks his wife for his tobacco, and enjoys a smoke (lines 438-53).
He travels on toward the tree, cutting various named, water-related features into the earth (lines 454-74).
Mugasha sees the tree apparently fleeing towards the south and proceeds in that direction, creating water sources as he goes (lines 475-83).
When he reaches the close vicinity of the tree he sends it first a butterfly-messenger then his named winds, which make the tree reel as though drunk (lines 484-98).
Mugasha then he approaches and plants his foot, creating more water sources as he does so (lines 499-510).

He grasps the tree, plucks it out, holds its roots up to the sun, and thrusts the branches downward into the earth (lines 511-16). Mugasha makes sure the tree will never grow again and then sets off to report to King Rugomora (lines 517-22).
Mugasha travels along toward Rugomora's palace, creating water sources, two rocky outcrops \'97 traditional locales for palaces, and a well-known impression of his foot in stone (lines 523-48).

He behaves recklessly at the court (lines 549-50) but creates another rocky outcrop for the royal aristocrats (lines 551-54). He continues on toward Lake Victoria, creating water sources as he goes (lines 555-75).

Mugasha passes through Rwija and knocks down another famous tree of sacrifices (lines 576-80.1). He continues toward the west, passing through landmark villages to the Kingdom of Karagwe, pausing at the king's palace at Bwelanyange to behave a bit recklessly and then continue on towards Rwanda (lines 582-97).
In Rwanda he creates the headwaters of the Kagera River and travels down it to his home in Lake Victoria (lines 598-609.1).
